memes.label: Admiral Mahan
memes.description: American naval officer and strategist (1840–1914), author of "The Influence of Sea Power upon History." In The Great Illusion, Mahan serves as the principal Anglo-Saxon interlocutor against whom Angell argues — a respected authority who defends armaments as necessary for national survival.

memes.label: The Admiralty
memes.description: The British government department responsible for the Royal Navy, led at the time of The Great Illusion by First Lord Winston Churchill, cited by Angell in the context of naval expenditure debates.

memes.label: Aggression
memes.description: The concept of "aggression" as the feared external threat that drives armament — and Angell's argument that modern economic conditions render interstate aggression irrational and historically rare.

memes.label: Alsace-Lorraine
memes.description: Territory seized by Germany in 1871 — Angell's example of annexation's limited economic value in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: American Civil War
memes.description: The 1861–1865 conflict Angell references in discussions of whether war can impose social institutions on a conquered population

memes.label: The North American Review
memes.description: American periodical founded in 1815, cited by Norman Angell in The Great Illusion for articles on international relations, the Spanish-American comparison, and the economic foundations of peace.

memes.label: Anglo-German Naval Race
memes.description: The naval arms race between Britain and Germany (1898–1914) that serves as the primary contemporary case study in The Great Illusion — a paradigmatic example of defensive arming producing mutual insecurity.

memes.label: Annexation
memes.description: The specific mechanism of conquest that Angell analyzes — the formal incorporation of enemy territory — demonstrating that even complete territorial absorption cannot transfer economic benefit to the conqueror

memes.label: Armament Race
memes.description: The practical manifestation of the Great Illusion — competitive military buildup driven by the false belief that national security and prosperity depend on superior armed force

memes.label: Australia
memes.description: Example of a colony that cannot be Germanized by conquest in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Austria
memes.description: Major European power whose interests Angell considers in the balance of power in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Balkan Wars
memes.description: The 1912–1913 conflicts that critics cited as disproving Angell's thesis — and which he argued confirmed it

memes.label: Balkans
memes.description: Region whose 1912 war critics used to challenge Angell's thesis in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Belgium
memes.description: A small neutral European state cited extensively by Angell as evidence that military power is unnecessary for national prosperity — Belgium had no significant military force yet enjoyed high bond ratings, thriving trade, and de facto security.

memes.label: Berlin
memes.description: Capital of the militarist power — whose financial vulnerability Angell exposes in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Boer War
memes.description: The 1899–1902 conflict Angell cites as evidence that colonial wars are economically self-defeating even for victors

memes.label: British Army
memes.description: Britain's land military force, referenced once by Norman Angell in The Great Illusion in a hypothetical comparison illustrating the primacy of domestic political conflict over international military threats.

memes.label: British Empire
memes.description: The global colonial and imperial system ruled by Great Britain. In The Great Illusion, the British Empire serves as Angell's central case study — an apparently successful conquest-state that on closer examination reveals the economic futility of political domination.

memes.label: British Government
memes.description: The executive government of the United Kingdom. In The Great Illusion, the British Government serves as both the principal actor in Angell's policy analysis and a recurrent example of how even well-intentioned governments perpetuate the armament illusion through institutional inertia.

memes.label: British Navy
memes.description: The Royal Navy, Britain's principal naval force and the world's largest fleet in the pre-1914 era. In The Great Illusion, Angell treats the British Navy as the paradigmatic case of how naval supremacy, far from being an economic necessity, had become an end in itself — sustained by institutional lobbies like the Navy League rather than rational strategic calculation.

memes.label: British Parliament
memes.description: The supreme legislative body of the United Kingdom. In The Great Illusion, the British Parliament appears in Angell's analysis of imperial governance — specifically, the limits of parliamentary sovereignty in protecting British economic interests abroad — as evidence that military power does not translate into effective control over commercial assets.

memes.label: Canada
memes.description: Case study in the impossibility of cultural conquest in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Colonies and Conquest
memes.description: Angell's application of the economic futility thesis to colonial possessions — demonstrating that colonies do not provide the economic benefits that imperialist doctrine assumes

memes.label: Congo Free State
memes.description: Angell's reference point for colonial exploitation critique

memes.label: The Contemporary Review
memes.description: British monthly periodical founded in 1866, cited by Norman Angell in The Great Illusion for an article by Dr. E.J. Dillon on German foreign policy.

memes.label: Crimean War
memes.description: Angell cites as example of the economic cost of war exceeding any conceivable benefit

memes.label: Daily Mail
memes.description: A British tabloid newspaper founded in 1896 by Alfred Harmsworth. In The Great Illusion, the Daily Mail serves as Angell's primary example of the jingoistic press that promotes militarism through sensationalized coverage of foreign threats.

memes.label: Defence
memes.description: The concept of "defence" as a justification for military armament — and Norman Angell's argument that the distinction between defence and aggression is logically incoherent when all nations arm for "defence."

memes.label: Disarmament
memes.description: The reduction or limitation of military armaments — presented by Norman Angell in The Great Illusion as the logical policy conclusion of his economic analysis, though he distinguishes his argument from the moral case for disarmament.

memes.label: Division of Labor
memes.description: The economic mechanism through which modern societies become interdependent — specialization of production across national boundaries makes nations reliant on one another's output

memes.label: Economic Interdependence
memes.description: The central thesis of The Great Illusion — that the economic and financial interdependence of modern civilized nations renders military conquest and political domination futile as means of national enrichment

memes.label: The Economist
memes.description: British weekly newspaper founded in 1843, cited once by Norman Angell in The Great Illusion for a letter by Douglas Owen on naval strategy and economics.

memes.label: England
memes.description: Central economic power in Angell's analysis in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Ernest Renan
memes.description: French philosopher (1823–1892) whose view of war as a necessary condition of civilization Angell groups with other nineteenth-century militarist thinkers

memes.label: Europe
memes.description: The theater of the Great Illusion — the civilization whose interdependence Angell maps in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Europe's Optical Illusion
memes.description: Norman Angell's 1909 pamphlet, the direct precursor to The Great Illusion, published obscurely in Europe before being expanded into the full-length book that launched the international debate.

memes.label: The Foreign Office
memes.description: The British government department responsible for foreign affairs, cited once by Norman Angell in The Great Illusion through an anecdote about a long-serving diplomat skeptical of German intentions.

memes.label: The Fortnightly Review
memes.description: Influential British periodical founded in 1865, cited by Norman Angell in The Great Illusion for an article by Benjamin Kidd illustrating the Social Darwinist assumptions Angell sought to refute.

memes.label: France
memes.description: Key historical example — defeated by Prussia but prospered economically in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Franco-Prussian War
memes.description: The 1870–71 war between France and Prussia, resulting in German unification and the annexation of Alsace-Lorraine. In The Great Illusion, Angell uses France's rapid post-war economic recovery to demonstrate that military conquest does not pay even when it appears to succeed.

memes.label: Frederic Harrison
memes.description: British Positivist philosopher and writer (1831–1923) whom Angell cites as an authority on the dangers of naval competition and the economic futility of military conquest

memes.label: French Government
memes.description: The government of the French Third Republic (1870–1940). In The Great Illusion, the French Government appears primarily as a comparative case — an example of how a state that had been the target of German conquest in 1870 nonetheless found its economic interests increasingly aligned with those of its former conqueror.

memes.label: Friedrich Nietzsche
memes.description: German philosopher (1844–1900) whose celebration of power and conflict Angell groups among the intellectual sources of militarist ideology

memes.label: Futility of Military Power
memes.description: Angell's central conclusion — that political and military power cannot achieve economic advantage or enforce cultural domination in the modern interdependent world

memes.label: General von Bernhardi
memes.description: German cavalry general and militarist writer (1849–1930) whose book Germany and the Next War (1912) Angell treats as the paradigmatic expression of the "Great Illusion" in its most candid form

memes.label: German Government
memes.description: The Imperial German Government under Kaiser Wilhelm II. In The Great Illusion, Angell examines the German Government not as a villain but as an institutional actor caught in the same structural logic of competitive armament as its British counterpart — a case study in how governments, regardless of intent, perpetuate the Great Illusion through the normal operation of the state system.

memes.label: German Navy
memes.description: The Imperial German Navy (Kaiserliche Marine), built up under Kaiser Wilhelm II and Admiral Tirpitz as a direct challenge to British naval supremacy. In The Great Illusion, the German Navy serves as Angell's exhibit of how naval expansion driven by prestige rather than economic logic creates a self-defeating cycle of competitive armament.

memes.label: German Unification
memes.description: The 1864–1871 process by which Prussia unified Germany through military force — the historical example Angell uses to show even successful conquest has limited returns

memes.label: Germany
memes.description: The archetypal militarist power whose ambitions Angell analyzes in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Hague Peace Conferences
memes.description: Angell's primary example of existing institutional machinery for peaceful settlement

memes.label: Helmuth von Moltke
memes.description: Prussian field marshal and military theorist (1800–1891) whom Angell cites as a paradigmatic spokesman for the view that war is a necessary and beneficial element of the human condition

memes.label: Herbert Spencer
memes.description: English philosopher and Social Darwinist (1820–1903) whose application of evolutionary concepts to society is critiqued by Angell as a key intellectual source of the "Great Illusion"

memes.label: Homer Lea
memes.description: American military writer (1876–1912) whose warnings about the Valley

memes.label: House of Commons
memes.description: The elected lower house of the British Parliament. In The Great Illusion, Angell draws on House of Commons debates — particularly speeches by the Speaker and by Sir Charles Dilke — to document how even the most sober parliamentary discourse was saturated with the assumptions of the armament system.

memes.label: Human Nature in Angell
memes.description: Angell's treatment of human nature as neither fixed nor irrelevant — the psychological dimension of war that must be understood and redirected rather than denied or accepted as immutable

memes.label: Imperialism
memes.description: The policy of extending a nation's power through territorial acquisition or political domination — analyzed by Norman Angell in The Great Illusion as economically self-defeating under modern conditions.

memes.label: India
memes.description: Colonial possession whose economic relationship with Britain Angell analyzes in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: International Arbitration
memes.description: The institutional mechanism Angell proposes as an alternative to war — a system of binding international adjudication to resolve disputes between nations without recourse to force

memes.label: Italian Unification
memes.description: Parallel case to German unification — nationalism and military force

memes.label: London
memes.description: The financial center whose credit networks Angell argues make conquest futile in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Maurice Block
memes.description: French statistician (1816–1901) cited by Angell on the economic interdependence of European nations

memes.label: Militarism
memes.description: The doctrine that military power is the ultimate guarantor of national security and prosperity — identified by Norman Angell in The Great Illusion as the central fallacy his book seeks to dismantle.

memes.label: Morning Post
memes.description: A Conservative British daily newspaper (1772–1937) frequently cited in The Great Illusion as a representative voice of imperialist and militarist editorial opinion, alongside the Daily Mail and The Times.

memes.label: Moroccan Crisis
memes.description: Pre-WWI diplomatic crisis illustrating Great Power rivalry

memes.label: Napoleonic Wars
memes.description: Angell contrasts Napoleonic-era conditions (when conquest could profit) with modern conditions

memes.label: National Prestige
memes.description: The psychological driver of imperial competition — the belief that a nation's standing in the world depends on its military power, which Angell identifies as a key mechanism sustaining the Great Illusion

memes.label: National Review
memes.description: A Conservative British monthly periodical (founded 1883) edited by Leo Maxse, frequently cited in The Great Illusion as a leading voice of militarist and imperialist opinion. Angell treats the National Review alongside Blackwood's and the Spectator as representative of the intellectual infrastructure that sustained the armament system.

memes.label: Nationality
memes.description: The identity basis of national sentiment — the sense of belonging to a distinct national community that Angell distinguishes from the emotion of patriotism and analyzes as a force that can either sustain or transcend the "Great Illusion"

memes.label: New York
memes.description: Financial center whose crises propagate through Angell's interdependent network in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: The Nineteenth Century
memes.description: A prestigious British monthly review (founded 1877 by James Knowles), later renamed The Nineteenth Century and After (1900). In The Great Illusion, Angell cites it as "the premier English review" and draws on contributions by figures such as Sir Harry Johnston and Prince Kropotkin — using the periodical as evidence that serious intellectual opinion, not merely tabloid sensationalism, sustained the militarist assumptions he sought to dismantle.

memes.label: Norman Angell
memes.description: Author of The Great Illusion (1910), British journalist, lecturer, and Nobel Peace Prize laureate (1933) who argued that military conquest is economically futile in the modern interdependent world.

memes.label: Otto von Bismarck
memes.description: Prussian statesman and first Chancellor of the German Empire (1815–1898), whom Angell uses as a key historical example of a conqueror who discovered the economic futility of military victory

memes.label: Paris
memes.description: Financial center interdependent with London, Berlin, and New York in Angell's analysis in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Patriotism
memes.description: The emotional attachment to nation that Angell analyzes as the primary psychological driver of war — distinct from, and often in tension with, rational economic self-interest

memes.label: Public Opinion
memes.description: The mechanism through which Angell believes the "Great Illusion" can be dispelled — an informed, critical public opinion that holds statesmen accountable to rational national interest rather than nationalist passion

memes.label: Reichstag
memes.description: The parliament of the German Empire (1871–1918). In The Great Illusion, the Reichstag appears as a case study in the limits of democratic control over military expenditure — a legislature that, despite representing a population with no interest in war, repeatedly approved naval budgets driven by the Kaiser's ambitions and the navy league's lobbying.

memes.label: Richard Cobden
memes.description: British manufacturer and Radical politician (1804–1865) whose free-trade internationalism is the intellectual tradition Angell's economic argument extends and systematizes

memes.label: Russia
memes.description: Great Power whose military competition with other European states Angell analyzes in *The Great Illusion*

memes.label: Russo-Japanese War
memes.description: Example of modern war's economic cost

memes.label: The Saturday Review
memes.description: British weekly periodical founded in 1855, cited once by Norman Angell in The Great Illusion as an example of the jingoistic press that shaped public opinion on the naval race.

memes.label: Spanish-American War
memes.description: Angell's era's example of imperial expansion

memes.label: The Spectator
memes.description: British weekly magazine and a leading voice of militarist and naval-supremacy advocacy in the pre-1914 period, extensively engaged with by Norman Angell in The Great Illusion.

memes.label: Struggle for Existence
memes.description: The Social Darwinist concept that Angell critiques — the claim that war between nations is a necessary and beneficial mechanism of evolutionary progress, which he argues is a misapplication of biological concepts to social phenomena

memes.label: The Credit Economy
memes.description: The specific financial form of modern wealth that makes it intangible and unconquerable — wealth based on credit, contract, and trust rather than physical assets

memes.label: The Great Illusion
memes.description: The titular fallacy of Norman Angell's book — the belief that nations can enrich themselves through military conquest, which persists despite being demonstrably false in the modern interdependent world

memes.label: The Great Illusion
memes.description: Norman Angell's 1910 treatise arguing that military conquest is economically futile in the modern interdependent world. Originally published as "Europe's Optical Illusion" (1909), expanded through multiple editions including a major 1933 revision.

memes.label: The Influence of Sea Power upon History
memes.description: Admiral Alfred Thayer Mahan's 1890 treatise on naval strategy, which argued that national greatness depends on maritime dominance. In The Great Illusion, this work represents the intellectual foundation of the armament philosophy that Angell seeks to refute.
aliases: The Influence of Sea Power upon History

memes.label: The Times
memes.description: The Times of London, Britain's leading establishment newspaper in the pre-1914 era. In The Great Illusion, Angell cites The Times alongside the Daily Mail and Morning Post as a representative voice of the British press consensus that systematically promoted militarist assumptions and naval expansion.

memes.label: Theodore Roosevelt
memes.description: American president (1858–1919) whose advocacy of the "strenuous life" and military virtues Angell cites as the American variant of the Great Illusion

memes.label: Thomas Baty
memes.description: English legal scholar (1869–1954) cited by Angell on international law and the limits of military force

memes.label: Treaty of Frankfurt
memes.description: The 1871 treaty ending the Franco-Prussian War, whose indemnity provisions Angell uses as the key case study in the economic futility of conquest

memes.label: War Indemnity
memes.description: The specific financial mechanism by which victors attempt to extract wealth from defeated nations — which Angell argues is economically self-defeating in the modern credit economy

memes.label: Wilhelm II
memes.description: German Emperor and King of Prussia (1859–1941, reigned 1888–1918) whose naval expansion program was the immediate political context for Angell's analysis of the Anglo-German rivalry

memes.label: William Lecky
memes.description: Irish historian (1838–1903) cited by Angell on the irrationality of patriotism
